name: The Fourteenth Landing
alias: the floor that isn't there · a lift that opens on a corridor your block does not have
body: First you notice the smell, because the smell arrives before the doors do: cold concrete after rain, and under it something sweeter, like joss ash gone damp, the way the void deck smells the morning after a wake has folded its tables away. The lift has been climbing too long. You pressed for the tenth, the way you always do, and the panel still shows ten lit in that tired amber, but the cabin keeps rising and the numbers on the display have stopped agreeing with the buttons. You feel it in your ears first, that small ascending pressure, the kind you swallow against on a fast lift, except this one was never fast; it is the rattling old kind with the dented steel and the mirror scuffed soft by decades of schoolbags and market trolleys. By the time the chime sounds it is a note you have never heard this lift make, lower, as if the cable were singing to itself. The doors part on a fourteenth landing, in a block that was built, like every block here, with no fourteen at all, the number skipped on the panel the same way four is skipped, politely, as though the building itself were observing the taboo.

It will not force you out; that is the rule everyone repeats. The fourteenth landing only waits, doors held open longer than the timer should allow, longer than the impatient morning crowd would ever tolerate, and shows you a corridor that is almost your corridor. The same scuffed letterbox grey, the same bicycle chained to the same railing, the same shoe rack with its slippers turned heel-out, but the unit numbers run in an order that means nothing, and the corridor light at the far end is on when every other tube is dark, humming that fluorescent hum that should have been replaced years ago. People say a figure stands at that lit end, patient, half-turned, as though it has been expecting whoever you are, as though it has stood through every National Day and every Hungry Ghost month waiting for the lift to bring it company. The way out is simple and absolute: you do not step over the gap. You press your own floor again, you keep your eyes on the rubber lip of the threshold, and you let the doors close on their own. To call out, to ask who is there, to apologise the way you would to a real neighbour you had startled, is to be counted as a neighbour, and neighbours are expected home.

What lingers is how ordinary the wrongness feels, how much it resembles coming home tired and not quite recognising your own block for a second, fumbling for the right lift lobby among three that look the same. People who claim the fourteenth landing say the figure never hurried, never reached, and that this was the worst part: it had all the time it needed, the way only the dead and the very old ever do. The detail that survives every retelling is the laundry. On the railing of that impossible corridor hangs one school uniform, pinned with green plastic pegs, sleeves heavy with water that has not dripped in years, a name tag turned to the wall so you can almost, but never quite, read it, and beside it a single canvas shoe, whitened with the same school chalk paste every child here has rubbed into their shoes on a Sunday night, set out to dry for a Monday that does not come.
note: This one circulates wherever the blocks are old enough to have skipped their fourth and fourteenth floors, which is to say almost everywhere, but the tellers favour the long flat estates of Toa Payoh, Ang Mo Kio, and the older Bedok rises. The night-shift cleaners trade it most, and the security uncles who do the 3am round and refuse, politely, to take the lift up alone. There are forum threads on the local paranormal boards, half of them swearing it is the eighth or thirteenth landing instead, which only tells you the number moves with the building.

The version told in the northern estates says you are safe so long as you never count the floors as you ride; counting is how the lift learns you can be counted. The ward most people trust is the dullest: face the doors, do not greet anyone, get out at the first real floor that offers itself and take the stairs the rest of the way. Likely invented by someone who simply hated old lifts, and yet every keeper I know still presses ten twice.
